metadata.title: The use of text‐based responses to improve our understanding and prediction of suicide risk

abstract:
Objective: Text-based responses may provide significant contributions to suicide risk prediction, yet research including text data is limited. This may be due to a lack of exposure and familiarity with statistical analyses for this data structure.

The current study provides an overview of data processing and statistical algorithms for text data, guided by an empirical example of 947 online participants

who completed both open-ended items and traditional self-report measures. We give an introduction to a number of text-based statistical approaches, including dictionary-based methods, topic modeling, word embeddings, and deep learning.

We analyze responses from the open-ended question "How do you feel today?", detailing characteristics of the responses, as well as predicting past-year suicidal ideation.

We see the analysis of text from social media, open-ended questions, and other text sources (i.e., medical records) as an important form of complementary assessment to traditional scales, shedding insight on what we are missing in our current set of questionnaires, which may ultimately serve to improve both our understanding and prediction of suicide.

sections.paper_id: Jacobucci et al. - 2021 - The use of text‐based responses to improve our understanding and prediction of suicide risk
paragraphs: Suicide risk prediction has remained stagnant over the past several decades (Franklin et al., 2017). One potential contributing factor is the reliance on traditional measurement strategies (i.e., Likert-type items/rating scales). Given the complexity of suicide, the standardized and restrictive response sets of traditional forms of measurement may be missing valuable information necessary to improve suicide risk prediction. Instead, focusing on the words people select to describe their experiences can shed a more nuanced light on one's momentary cognitive and affective states (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010), including those that may contribute to suicide risk. Text-based responses have been shown to reflect processes an individual may be unable (Boyd & Pennebaker, 2017) or unwilling (Drum, Brownson, Burton Denmark, & Smith, 2009) to explicitly acknowledge or discuss. In light of these advantages, researchers have recently turned to the analysis of text responses, largely acquired from social media (Bryan et al., 2018;Luxton, June, & Fairall, 2012), such as Twitter or microblogs (Cheng, Li, Kwok, Zhu, & Yip, 2017;Coppersmith, Leary, Crutchley, & Fine, 2018;Du et al., 2018;Guan, Hao, Cheng, Yip, & Zhu, 2015), or third-party text (e.g., hospital discharge notes; McCoy, Castro, Roberson, Snapper, & Perlis, 2016), as a way to capture the multifaceted processes of suicide risk. Findings from these studies highlight the promise of text to provide incremental information in suicide risk prediction beyond existing risk assessment measures.

Text data relevant to suicide risk are widely available, yet the overall utilization of such data has been minimal. Even fewer researchers have utilized active data collection of text-based responses (Cook et al., 2016). A contributing factor may be that the statistical approaches necessary for text data have received limited attention and development within psychological research (see Rohrer, Brümmer, Schmukle, Goebel, & Wagner, 2017). Similarly, there are minimal resources available that guide researchers in the incorporation of text within quantitative study designs. The current paper aims to address this gap. The goal is not to call for an abandonment of traditional risk measures, but rather to expose researchers to a data collection methodology that may allow us, as a field, to better consider questions, such as: What relevant proximal or distal elements of risk are we missing to better understand suicide? Are respondents more precise in detailing their background, thoughts, behaviors, or other elements of life when not constrained in their mode of response? And are respondents more likely to disclose aspects of their experience when not directly asked? These questions serve as motivation in providing a brief overview of the statistical algorithms available for analyzing text responses, with a focus on producing interpretable associations based on text responses either on their own or in relation to distal outcomes or predictors. To accomplish this goal, we discuss text data collection, cleaning, and preprocessing and statistical algorithms designed for text data; then, we highlight an empirical application in suicide research. We do not aim to be comprehensive in the level of detail or scope, instead directing readers to articles and/or books that are more thorough.

paragraphs: The choice of active versus passive text data collection requires consideration. Responding to open-ended questions (versus Likert-type) allows for flexibility and the influence of theory in questions asked, but carries a higher degree of burden, which can influence respondents' willingness to provide text responses (Andrews, 2005;Kjell, Kjell, Garcia, & Sikström, 2018;Scholz & Zuell, 2012); this severely reduces the number of questions that can be asked within a single study. On the other hand, several methods exist for scraping data from social media or the web (see Landers, Brusso, Cavanaugh, & Collmus, 2016), giving researchers access to a large sample of text-based responses. However, data can be limited at the participant level, specifically with respect to theoretically relevant aspects of suicidal thinking or behavior.

Our understanding of sample size requirements for text data and associated algorithms is underdeveloped. This can be partially attributed to the new area of inquiry, but is also impacted by a large number of factors beyond the number of participants: type of analysis, number of words, and quality or richness of responses. To our knowledge, there has not been quantitative work directly examining the influence of sample size on text algorithms in social and behavioral research. In machine learning more broadly (which several text algorithms are considered to be), it is well understood that a larger sample size facilitates more complex modeling of the data (e.g., see Yarkoni & Westfall, 2017). Given this, collecting data at a larger scale from social media may be more fruitful for prediction, as well as for more intricate modeling of the words individuals use (see Deep Learning section).

paragraphs: Simply put, a large amount of text is generally misspelled.

Identifying misspellings can be challenging and time-consuming; specific software, however, has been created to identify misspelled words and offer suggestions for replacement (Ooms, 2017). Beyond correcting spelling, it is common to transform all words into lower case and remove punctuation to further reduce irregularities across the text. These are necessary data cleaning steps because if these corrections are not made, then each misspelled word (or those with different punctuation) would be treated as distinct, which can significantly reduce performance of later analyses.

paragraphs: To further reduce text variability, words that share a common "stem" can be mapped into the same word (i.e., stemming). For instance, the words walking and walked can be transformed into their stem, walk. For most algorithms that use single words as tokens (described below), stemming reduces the number of words to be modeled and increases model clarity (given the common uses of many same-stem words).

paragraphs: Different text algorithms can require different representations of words. The simplest way to represent words is as a bag of words (BOW). In this representation, the sequence in which the words were used does not matter ("I care" is treated the same as "care I").

An additional step in determining the representation of words via BOW model is tokenization, or the process of splitting the text into "tokens," which can be individual words, sequences of words, or individual sentences. The most common form of tokenization is the ngram representation. An n-gram is a contiguous sequence of n items from a given sequence of words (sentence), including unigram (most common), bigram, trigram, four-gram, or five-gram. For example, using bigrams, the phrase "I like myself" can be broken into "I like" and "like myself."

The choice of a unigram, bigram, trigram, or more complex representation is application-specific and data-dependent. While, in theory, a richer model of text can be created using a more complex representation such as trigrams, there are two major limitations that often prohibit such complexity. First, text is inherently sparse-any given word or, in the case of trigrams, three-word sequence will tend to occur relatively infrequently across a set of responses. This sparsity increases as the complexity of tokenization increases from unigrams to n-grams, making the modeling of associations among tokens increasingly more challenging. This problem is compounded in the case of short responses. Second, most algorithms for modeling text are computationally intensive. Because computational complexity scales with the complexity of tokenization, the use of unigrams is often necessary because it reduces the computational burden.
Therefore, the choice of word representation should be guided by consideration of these trade-offs.

paragraphs: There are several statistical algorithms that have been developed for use with text data. Below, we provide an overview of dictionarybased approaches, topic modeling, embeddings, and deep learning.

Each algorithm approaches text in a different manner, directly influencing data interpretation. Researchers may wish to choose one algorithm over another based on the research question at hand. Given the popularity and availability of resources related to dictionarybased approaches, we devote more attention to (and provide empirical examples for) topic modeling, embeddings, and deep learning.

paragraphs: One of the simplest dictionary-based approaches is sentiment analysis, which assesses whether responses are positive, neutral, or negative. To accomplish this, one of several general-purpose lexicons based on unigrams (available in software such as R) is selected to assign sentiment scores to each word. For example, the AFINN lexicon (Nielsen, 2011) assigns scores ranging from -5 to 5 (i.e., greater negativity to greater positivity), where the word abhorrent would receive a score of -3 and sympathy would receive a score of 2. A total sentiment score for a document can be calculated, for example, by averaging the word sentiment scores. Sentiment analysis has been applied in the context of suicide notes (Pestian et al., 2012), as well as in the analysis of self-harm posts on social media (Fu, Cheng, Wong, & Yip, 2013), among others.

paragraphs: Of dictionary-based approaches, psychologists may be most familiar with Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC; Pennebaker, Francis, & Booth, 2001), which is implemented as a commercially available software program (Pennebaker, Boyd, Jordan, & Blackburn, 2015).

LIWC analyzes text according to a dictionary comprised of 6400 words, word stems, and emoticons, largely related to psychological processes (e.g., affective, social processes; Pennebaker et al., 2015).

From this analysis, users get an output of scores on 90 components of the dictionary and linguistic/grammatical dimensions of their data. In the context of suicide research, LIWC has been applied to word use of poet suicide decedents (Stirman & Pennebaker, 2001), content of suicide notes (Handelman & Lester, 2007), and suicide risk prediction in Chinese microblog users (Zhang, Huang, Liu, Li, Chen, & Zhu, 2014). Recent research has also focused on analyzing text in message forums (e.g., Van den, Nest, Till, & Niederkrotenthaler, 2018) and text messages (e.g., Nobles, Glenn, Kowsari, Teachman, & Barnes, 2018).

If researchers are interested in only specific psychological processes, particularly a dimension not captured by LIWC, there is also the option to create a user-defined dictionary. In contrast to manually assessing each response, one can define a dictionary (e.g., only negative emotion words) and then use an automated script to calculate the frequency of category use across each observation (typically divided by total response length for each individual to control for wordier responses).

paragraphs: One of the earliest and most popular approaches in psychology is latent semantic analysis (LSA) (also known as latent semantic indexing; Deerwester et al., 1990;Landauer, Foltz, & Laham, 1998). LSA represents text as a matrix of document-word co-occurrence counts (e.g., how often does the word "hate" appear in the same response as "fear"). This information is then mapped to a lower-dimensional structure with singular value decomposition, a process analogous to principal component analysis. Each word is represented as a loading on the prespecified number of dimensions, with two words' closeness in space representing their degree of semantic similarity. We note that in contrast to other forms of topic models, LSA is not necessarily a model, but rather a dimension reduction technique that can yield interpretable summaries.

paragraphs: One of the most common forms of topic models is latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003). LDA assumes that each word across documents can be modeled by a mixture, or combination, of latent topics. With a preset number of topics, each word is assumed to follow a categorical distribution corresponding to one of the K topics. The topic associated with each word is also assumed to follow a categorical distribution parameterized by a vector of probabilities, constrained to sum to one, that is assumed to follow a Dirichlet distribution. In both LSA and LDA, the topics derived do not have an inherent meaning (just as in factor analysis) but require labeling through an examination of the words most representative of each topic. In comparison with LSA, LDA has been found to generally produce more coherent topics (Stevens, Kegelmeyer, Andrzejewski, & Buttler, 2012). For this reason, and because of its popularity, we spend more time discussing LDA and its extensions.

In LDA, it is typically necessary to test a sequence of possible number of topics, using some measure of fit to choose a final model. This form of LDA is an unsupervised model, similar to a latent class model, as there is not an explicit outcome in the model.

An extension of LDA is the correlated topic model (Blei & Lafferty, 2006), which jointly estimates the correlation among topicsfoot_1 , providing an additional layer of information for understanding the meaning of each topic. One of the main benefits of LDA is its degree of interpretability. After running an LDA model with a prespecified number of topics, each topic can then be interpreted with respect to the words with the highest probabilities of representation. We note that a similar process holds for LSA.

Additionally, researchers can assess the document-by-topic matrix which depicts the degree to which each document is represented by each topic. This form of assessment is particularly important to ensure that a small number of documents (participants) are not driving the formation of a topic, a result which can be driven by invalid responses (e.g., in a different language). The documenttopic matrix can also be used to compare documents with respect to similarity or dissimilarity. In the context of suicide research, for example, the topic representation of a response associated with a history of suicide attempt could be used to find similar responses that may share a higher propensity for suicidal behavior. In contrast to unsupervised LDA, multiple extensions have been developed to incorporate outcomes and/or covariates in the model.

For the incorporation of outside variables, supervised LDA (sLDA; Mcauliffe & Blei, 2008) was developed to extract topics that are informative in predicting an outcome. As opposed to solely extracting latent topics (unsupervised LDA) and then using other analyses to predict an outcome, the sLDA model has shown superior predictive performance (Mcauliffe & Blei, 2008;Zhu, Ahmed, & Xing, 2012). A second extension is the structured topic model (STM). This model allows for the inclusion of predictors of the latent topics (Roberts et al., 2014), providing additional description of each topic. Finally, a more recent development includes covariates in a supervised topic model (sLDAX; Wilcox, Jacobucci, & Zhang, in preparation). Results compare the conditional effects of the latent topics on an outcome, given the inclusion of covariates.
Notably, using sLDAX, researchers can examine the incremental validity of topics above and beyond traditional self-report measures (discussed below).

paragraphs: As noted, an important aspect of using topic models is selecting the number of topics. There have been several methods created for this purpose, with one of the most common being perplexity (Blei, Ng, & Jordan, 2003). Assessed on a holdout set of documents, perplexity (for a preset number of topics) compares the theoretical word distribution to the actual word distribution, with lower scores indicating better generalization. Despite its popularity, prior research has found weak relationships between perplexity and human judgment of topic coherence (Chang, Gerrish, Wang, Boyd-Graber, & Blei, 2009). Similar to structural equation modeling, where a host of fit metrics have been createdfoot_2 and no single metric works best across applications, there is no optimal way to select the number of topics. Statistical assessments should first be used to hone in on a small number of topics; then, domain expertise should guide the final model selection. In sLDA models, on the other hand, there are multiple layers of interpretation: the LDA model and the regression coefficients. The most common interpretation is based on the words with the highest loadings on each topic in combination with the regression coefficients. See the Motivating Example for an application of utilizing perplexity in addition to sLDA model interpretation.

paragraphs: Given that the number of unique words used across responses can be quite large, the dimensionality can be reduced using word embeddings. Word embeddings map individual words to a lower-dimensional space, with the additional benefit of transforming categorical representations of words to a continuous embedding representation (e.g., instead of representing each document as a count of 80 distinct words, it can be represented as continuous scores of 40 embedding dimensions). This is accomplished using pretrained embeddings (e.g., word2vec) or through training one's own embeddings as a part of a larger neural network model. In contrast to treating each word as just a unigram or bigram (as in dictionary-based approaches) and not taking into account the surrounding words, embeddings often use a skip-gram modelfoot_3 (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, & Dean, 2013). This compares the target word to a vector of sampled context words. As an example, we will use a portion of the sentence from our Motivating Example (below): "have a sinking feeling in my gut." If we select "feeling" as our target word and a skip window (i.e., number of words to consider left and right of target) of three, we would include "have," "a," and "sinking" to the left and "in," "my," and "gut" to the right. From this, the algorithm will randomly sample other words from that document that act as distractors and attempt to predict which words fall into the skip window versus which are distractors. Using this algorithm across the length of the documents provides a context vector for each word, defining the similarity between two words, or more simply put, how likely two words were to co-occur. Using one of the most popular embedding algorithms, a generalization of the original word2vec algorithm (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, & Dean, 2013), termed Glove (Pennington, Socher, & Manning, 2014), our selected target word "feeling," produced high similarity scores for "i," "today," and "good," when considering the full document. LDA has been recently integrated with embeddings (e.g., Dieng, Ruiz, & Blei, 2019), both in using topics to create embeddings and identifying latent topics directly from embeddings; consequently, the LDA model can better incorporate the context of each word, moving beyond an unordered representation of words 4 .

One newer area of inquiry is incorporating domain-specific information into the embedding algorithm, as pretrained models using general vocabularies may not translate well to domain-specific tasks (Blitzer, Dredze, & Pereira, 2007). Given that word2vec and Glove were pretrained on a domain-general corpus of text, questions have been raised with respect to their application to domain-specific text (Sarma, Liang, & Sethares, 2018). In Sarma, Liang, and Sethares

Given that the number of unique words used across responses can be quite large, the dimensionality can be reduced using word embeddings. Word embeddings map individual words to a lower-dimensional space, with the additional benefit of transforming categorical representations of words to a continuous embedding representation (e.g., instead of representing each document as a count of 80 distinct words, it can be represented as continuous scores of 40 embedding dimensions). This is accomplished using pretrained embeddings (e.g., word2vec) or through training one's own embeddings as a part of a larger neural network model. In contrast to treating each word as just a unigram or bigram (as in dictionary-based approaches) and not taking into account the surrounding words, embeddings often use a skip-gram modelfoot_3 (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, & Dean, 2013). This compares the target word to a vector of sampled context words. As an example, we will use a portion of the sentence from our Motivating Example (below): "have a sinking feeling in my gut." If we select "feeling" as our target word and a skip window (i.e., number of words to consider left and right of target) of three, we would include "have," "a," and "sinking" to the left and "in," "my," and "gut" to the right. From this, the algorithm will randomly sample other words from that document that act as distractors and attempt to predict which words fall into the skip window versus which are distractors. Using this algorithm across the length of the documents provides a context vector for each word, defining the similarity between two words, or more simply put, how likely two words were to co-occur. Using one of the most popular embedding algorithms, a generalization of the original word2vec algorithm (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, & Dean, 2013), termed Glove (Pennington, Socher, & Manning, 2014), our selected target word "feeling," produced high similarity scores for "i," "today," and "good," when considering the full document. LDA has been recently integrated with embeddings (e.g., Dieng, Ruiz, & Blei, 2019), both in using topics to create embeddings and identifying latent topics directly from embeddings; consequently, the LDA model can better incorporate the context of each word, moving beyond an unordered representation of words 4 .

One newer area of inquiry is incorporating domain-specific information into the embedding algorithm, as pretrained models using general vocabularies may not translate well to domain-specific tasks (Blitzer, Dredze, & Pereira, 2007). Given that word2vec and Glove were pretrained on a domain-general corpus of text, questions have been raised with respect to their application to domain-specific text (Sarma, Liang, & Sethares, 2018). In Sarma, Liang, and Sethares

sections.paper_id: Jacobucci et al. - 2021 - The use of text‐based responses to improve our understanding and prediction of suicide risk
paragraphs: The term deep learning is typically used to refer to neural networks that have more than one hidden layer, which are layers of latent nodes that link observed predictors to an observed outcome. A simple neural network model (containing two hidden layers) is displayed in Figure 1; this model maps the input (X) with nonlinear activation functions through the hidden layers to an output (Y). Although there are a number of different types of deep learning models available for application in a wide range of domains, we briefly describe four of the most popular for text data. In these models, embeddings can either be directly learned as a part of training the deep learning model or pretrained embeddings can be used directly as input.

Recent applied research compared several approaches for using text from emergency department clinical notes to predict mental status, finding a deep learning model with embeddings outperforming other machine learning algorithms paired with a bag-of-words representation (Obeid et al., 2019).

Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are similar to autoregressive processes, where the modeling of a word (i.e., word t ) takes into account the prior word (i.e., word t-1 ) as a mechanism for considering the sequential (time-ordered) nature of responses. Going back to our example response ("have a sinking feeling in my gut"), this mean that the word "feeling" also includes weight from the previous word "sinking." This allows for a better differentiation between positive versus negative terms, which is not the case for previously discussed algorithms (unless modeling as bigrams).

A more complex form of RNNs is the use of long short-term memory (LSTM; Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) networks. Where simple RNNs have difficulty retaining information from more than a few time steps (words) prior, LSTM networks were developed to address this through a more explicit memory and forgetting processes.

The processing of each word t can include information from many words prior. However, to model transitions to new sentences or disregard irrelevant information, the model also includes a forgetting operation. Beyond the LSTM variant of RNNs, there is a multitude of more advanced modifications (e.g., Transformers; Devlin, Chang, Lee, & Toutanova, 2018;Radford et al., 2019) which all model the sequence of words leading up to target word t 5 .

There are additional variants of neural networks that have also been used for predicting outcomes or generating text, such as convolutional networks (e.g., Kim, 2014). Additional, neural networks exist for unsupervised (no Y) tasks, such as autoencoders (e.g., Liou, Cheng, Liou, & Liou, 2014) that perform a form of dimension reduction (often to remove noise from images), as well as embeddings.

However, neural networks (deep learning) are a more general architecture, which often include multiple layers (i.e., stack) to form deep models. An important drawback to such complex models of language is the need for large datasets.

sections.paper_id: Jacobucci et al. - 2021 - The use of text‐based responses to improve our understanding and prediction of suicide risk
paragraphs: To demonstrate the use of statistical algorithms for text data, we recruited participants through the online platform Amazon Mechanical Turk (mTurk; n = 947; mean age = 32.74). As opposed to using secondary data sources (i.e., social media), we opted to collect data on theoretically driven and empirically utilized open-ended questions.

For the purposes of this paper, we utilize data from two questions, "How do you feel today?" and "How connected do you feel to others?", with a primary focus on the former given its prior use in research on suicide risk (Cook et al., 2016). After manually screening the text responses to remove those that were not in English, were generated from bots, gave one-or two-word responses, or had copied and pasted responses from websites, we had reduced the sample size to 893. We note that manual inspection was necessary given that programming-based screening missed careless or inauthentic responses. Participants also completed three traditional self-report 4 In preparing a word matrix for LDA modeling, the word order does not matter as it is not incorporated into the model. 5 There are bidirectional variants which take into account words that occur after word t . Ringer et al., 2018;Van Orden et al., 2012), in addition to a face-valid question about their experience of suicidal ideation. Our target outcome was past-year suicidal ideation (1) as compared to no past-year ideation (0), which included those with suicidal ideation more than one year ago (but not in the past year).

paragraphs: Of the total sample, 280 participants reported past-year suicidal ideation (target outcome). For our primary text question of, "How do you feel today?", the level of detail provided across responses varied widely, with a range of three -187 words (mean =38.4 words). As The key aspect of applying text algorithms to model these responses is not only to capture different forms of distress reported (i.e., broader distress versus specific stressors), but to also differentiating between more global (general feelings of anxiety) and local responses (anxiety related to taking a test today). Data were preprocessed using stemming, removing punctuation and numbers, and correcting misspellings, in addition to removing stop words (e.g., a, the, and) for the analysis in LDA. This preprocessing reduced the average number of words to 11.8, highlighting the shortness of the average response.

paragraphs: Utilizing responses from the question, "How do you feel today?", we first provide a demonstration of using perplexity to select topics within an LDA model. Choosing the number of topics in LDA (and extensions) can be challenging. The results from calculating perplexity with 5-fold cross-validation on two to fifteen topics are displayed in Figure 2. As demonstrated, there is a high degree of variability to the perplexity values for each number of topics, making it difficult to distinguish between the models. We attribute this mostly to the shortness of responses.

For the sLDA models, the Akaike information criterion (AIC; Akaike, 1974) is calculated as a mechanism to choose the number of topics. From this, we selected five topics to predict past-year suicidal ideation, which resulted in an area under receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.54 (assessed with fivefold cross-validation). We also consider the interpretation of the sLDA model. To do this, we review both words with the highest representation on each topic and the associated regression coefficients, displayed in

paragraphs: There are several ways to assess the relationship between scores derived from text data and traditional forms of assessment. Of these, the simplest is to create dimension scores, such as from topic models (i.e., LDA 7 ) or outputted variables from dictionary-based ap-proach (i.e., LIWC), and then utilize these scores as variables in a secondary analysis (which also includes data from traditional assessments). Researchers can examine the extracted scores from multiple text algorithms, such as assessing the relationship between LIWC emotion scores and extracted LDA topics that are thought to capture elements of emotional experience. For methods such as LDA, this approach can result in a loss in information (similar to extracting factor scores versus using structural equation modeling), which has spawned a number of LDA extensions that integrate external variables (i.e., sLDA and STM). This aspect of analysis may be informative as the associations between traditional assessment scores and those from text can demonstrate the validity of the text scores. For instance, recent research found that LIWC emotion scores were not significantly associated with self-reported state emotion experience scores (Sun et al., 2019).

For methods such as LDA, this approach can result in a loss in information (similar to extracting factor scores versus using structural equation modeling), which has spawned a number of LDA extensions that integrate external variables (i.e., sLDA and STM). This aspect of analysis may be informative as the associations between traditional assessment scores and those from text can demonstrate the validity of the text scores. For instance, recent research found that LIWC emotion scores were not significantly associated with self-reported state emotion experience scores (Sun et al., 2019).

paragraphs: As we do not view text data as replacing traditional forms of assessment, but instead a viable complement, we review the examination of text providing potential incremental validity to the prediction of suicide outcomes. This is assessed through the joint modeling of a suicide outcome (i.e., past-year suicidal ideation)

with both text data and traditional risk factor assessment. By using both types of variables in the regression equation, we can assess the conditional effects of each variable, allowing us to examine whether text-based responses add to the prediction of our particular outcome.

paragraphs: To provide an example of examining incremental validity, we calculated the mean sentiment scores from the question, "How do you feel today?". In examining just this question as a predictor of past- As a final example, we predicted past-year suicidal ideation while simultaneously using the depression, anxiety, and stress subscale scores, in addition to hopelessness scores, along with three topics using an sLDAX model. Scores on the depression subscale were a significant predictor of past-year suicidal ideation (b = 0.35, 95% BCI:

[0.12, 0.58]). Further, though the AUC remained unchanged from the covariate-only model, the topics provided interpretable summaries of responses to "How do you feel today?" and a measure of association between topic use and past-year suicidal thoughts. In this model, one topic-negative mental, physical, and financial distress (e.g., "tired," "stressed," "work," "anxious," "money," "overwhelmed," "pain," and "body")-was positively, but not statistically significantly, associated with suicidal ideation (b = 0.34, 95% BCI: [-0.24, 0.91]).

On the other hand, the topic most negatively associated with suicidal ideation (b = -0.28, 95% BCI: [-0.85, 0.31]) consisted of positive emotions (e.g., "excited," "energetic," and "hopeful") and spirituality (e.g., "blessed" and "God"). The third topic that was the least strongly associated with suicidal ideation (b = -0.07, 95% BCI: [-0.64, 0.51])
consisted of descriptions of daily routines (e.g., "woke," "sleep,"
"week," "home," "dinner," and "weekend").

sections.paper_id: Jacobucci et al. - 2021 - The use of text‐based responses to improve our understanding and prediction of suicide risk
paragraphs: Text data may provide advantages in the prediction of suicide risk.
There are numerous approaches to the analysis of text data, each with different characteristics of the information extracted from text.

This ranges from relatively simple methods, such as sentiment analysis that extracts valence scores from each response, to deep learning, which can model the sequence in which individuals use words. Topic modeling is a middle ground that has similarities with methods such as principal components and factor analysis. Within topic modeling, we detailed LDA, an unsupervised approach to model the responses, as well as sLDA and sLDAX, supervised approaches which both model the text responses and also include regression coefficients to predict an outcome of interest. We detailed an example of LDA and deep learning to expose readers to potentially new, or unfamiliar, algorithms.

One of the main limitations in our empirical examples is the amount of text data collected from each participant. Although our sample size was relatively large, length of response is an important factor and most likely led to diminished power to detect effects and improvement in AUC values. For instance, in the sLDAX results, it is likely that topic regression coefficients are attenuated due to the shortness of the responses (Wilcox et al., in preparation 8 ); we expect that with longer responses, these effects will be larger, and more precisely, estimated. Across our analyses, a number of the algorithms (sLDA, sLDAX, neural networks) were overly complex for the amount of information contained in the data, which led to simpler approaches (sentiment analysis) evidencing better fit. We strongly believe that using text data will improve our prediction of suicide outcomes, either used solely or in conjunction with alternative types of data; however, this will require either longer responses (combining responses from multiple questions)

or from larger sample sizes (EHR notes). Given this, we recommend researchers to focus on both sample size and response length when collecting data, of which we recommend sample sizes greater than 1,000 and response lengths greater than 50 words (after cleaning and removing stop words). Of note, we believe that deficiencies in one aspect of data collection can be complemented by increases in the other (e.g., a sample size of 300, but respondents write multiple-page autobiographical essays).
Going back to the three questions we posed in the introduction,

we cannot fully answer these with just one text question (using only one for pedagogical purposes), instead it would be necessary to integrate responses from other open-ended questions or other text sources. In collecting data, researchers are not limited to just using data from one source, such as just social media posts. As an example
